request.question: Robert Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with a sportscaster who has been an anchor for ESPN since what year ?

title: College Football Scoreboard
Robert Flores
Jay Crawford
ESPN Classic Remembers
Chris McKendry
Dana Jacobson
Jay Harris (sportscaster)
John Buccigross
Chris Berman
Scott Van Pelt
sentences: College Football Scoreboard is a program on ESPN and ESPN2 that provides up to the minute scores and highlights during the college football season.
The official name is "College Football Scoreboard presented by Acura".
The name of the show was College "Gameday" Scoreboard up until 2006.
It airs four times a day at 3 p.m. ET and 7 p.m. ET on ESPN and at 3 p.m. ET and at 7 p.m. ET on ESPN2.
The 3 p.m. ET programs on both networks are thirty minutes long and the 7 p.m. ET programs on both networks lead up to "College Football Primetime".
However, it is subject to, and often pre-empted due to earlier games running long into the show's timeslot, and often games run into each other without any kind of "Scoreboard" interlude.
Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.
Flores formerly worked at ESPN.
Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).
Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.
He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".
Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.
He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.
Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.
Jason "Jay" Crawford (born July 4, 1965) is an American sports journalist who was formerly employed by ESPN.
Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of "SportsCenter" with Chris McKendry until April 2017.

Prior to that, Crawford spent nine years hosting ESPN's morning show "Cold Pizza" and its successor "ESPN First Take", as well as "Cold Pizza"<nowiki>'</nowiki>s spinoff series "1st and 10" until its 2011 cancellation.
ESPN Classic Remembers is a television program on ESPN Classic that chronicles historic games and events, personalities and other sports milestones.
The show debuted on September 11, 2006 and is hosted by ESPN "SportsCenter" personality Chris McKendry.
It is primarily a thirty-minute program followed by a rerun of the sporting event it is chronicling.
Chris McKendry (born Christine McKendry February 18, 1968) is a journalist for ESPN, a role she has served since 1996.
She was co-anchor of the 11-1pm ET weekday block of live ESPN "SportsCenter" shows, alongside Jay Crawford.
As of April 1, 2016, she serves as full-time on-site host for ESPN tennis coverage of the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon and US Open.
Dana Jacobson (born November 5, 1971) is a sports anchorwoman for CBS.
She joined the network in 2013 after ten years at ESPN, where she began as an ESPNEWS anchor in December 2002 and soon became a regular anchor on the 6 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter".
In March 2005, she was named co-host of "Cold Pizza", and transitioned with the show as it became "ESPN First Take".
Jacobson provided sideline reporting for ESPN's coverage of NBA Sunday night games.
On Monday, December 26, 2011, Jacobson announced that Friday, December 30, 2011, would be her final day on "First Take."
She returned to anchoring SportsCenter shortly thereafter.
On March 27, 2012, USA Today announced that Jacobson would leave ESPN when her contract expires at the end of April.
Monday, April 30, 2012 was her final day at ESPN when she anchored the 6-8 p.m. ET SportsCenter show.
Jacobson joined CBS Sports and the cast of CBS Sports Radio's morning show.
"TBD in the AM" in January 2013, co-hosting along with Tiki Barber and Brandon Tierney.
On December 5, 2014, Dana tweeted out that she would be leaving "TBD in the AM" to work on CBS Sports Network.
Jay Harris (born February 22, 1965 in Norfolk, Virginia) is an American journalist who has worked for ESPN since February 2003.
Jay currently calls late night his home, seen primarily on the 11pm and midnight eastern editions of SportsCenter, and sometimes on the west coast Sportscenter from Los Angeles.

Jay has hosted a variety of shows during his tenure at ESPN, including Sportscenter, Outside The Lines, NFL Live, Baseball Tonight, Cold Pizza, First Take, Friday Night Fights, and ESPN Sports Saturday on ABC.
John Buccigross ( ) is an American sportscaster.
He has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996.
Christopher James Berman (born May 10, 1955), nicknamed Boomer, is an American sportscaster.
He has been an anchor for "SportsCenter" on ESPN since 1979, joining a month after its initial launch, and hosted the network's "Sunday NFL Countdown" program from 1985 to 2016.
He has also anchored "Monday Night Countdown", U.S. Open golf, the Stanley Cup Finals, and other programming on ESPN and ABC Sports.
Berman calls play-by-play of select Major League Baseball games for ESPN, which included the Home Run Derby until 2016.

A six-time honoree of the National Sports Media Association's "National Sportscaster of the Year" award, Berman was instrumental in establishing ESPN's lasting popularity during the network's formative years.
He is well known for his various catchphrases and quirky demeanor.
In January 2017, it was announced that Berman would be stepping down from several NFL-related roles at ESPN, but would be remaining at the company.
Scott Van Pelt is an American sportscaster and sports talk show host.
He co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter" on ESPN, served as the co-host of "SVP & Russillo" alongside Ryen Russillo on ESPN Radio, and hosts various golf events for the network.
In June 2015, Van Pelt left his radio show to become a solo anchor for a midnight edition of "SportsCenter".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).  Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.  He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".  Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.  He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.  Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.

She was co-anchor of the 11-1pm ET weekday block of live ESPN "SportsCenter" shows, alongside Jay Crawford.  As of April 1, 2016, she serves as full-time on-site host for ESPN tennis coverage of the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon and US Open.

Jay Crawford | Jason "Jay" Crawford (born July 4, 1965) is an American sports journalist who was formerly employed by ESPN.  Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of "SportsCenter" with Chris McKendry until April 2017.  Prior to that, Crawford spent nine years hosting ESPN's morning show "Cold Pizza" and its successor "ESPN First Take", as well as "Cold Pizza"<nowiki>'</nowiki>s spinoff series "1st and 10" until its 2011 cancellation.

Chris Berman | Christopher James Berman (born May 10, 1955), nicknamed Boomer, is an American sportscaster.  He has been an anchor for "SportsCenter" on ESPN since 1979, joining a month after its initial launch, and hosted the network's "Sunday NFL Countdown" program from 1985 to 2016.  He has also anchored "Monday Night Countdown", U.S. Open golf, the Stanley Cup Finals, and other programming on ESPN and ABC Sports.  Berman calls play-by-play of select Major League Baseball games for ESPN, which included the Home Run Derby until 2016.  A six-time honoree of the National Sports Media Association's "National Sportscaster of the Year" award, Berman was instrumental in establishing ESPN's lasting popularity during the network's formative years.  He is well known for his various catchphrases and quirky demeanor.  In January 2017, it was announced that Berman would be stepping down from several NFL-related roles at ESPN, but would be remaining at the company.

Scott Van Pelt | Scott Van Pelt is an American sportscaster and sports talk show host.  He co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter" on ESPN, served as the co-host of "SVP & Russillo" alongside Ryen Russillo on ESPN Radio, and hosts various golf events for the network.  In June 2015, Van Pelt left his radio show to become a solo anchor for a midnight edition of "SportsCenter".

Jay Harris (sportscaster) | Jay Harris (born February 22, 1965 in Norfolk, Virginia) is an American journalist who has worked for ESPN since February 2003.  Jay currently calls late night his home, seen primarily on the 11pm and midnight eastern editions of SportsCenter, and sometimes on the west coast Sportscenter from Los Angeles.  Jay has hosted a variety of shows during his tenure at ESPN, including Sportscenter, Outside The Lines, NFL Live, Baseball Tonight, Cold Pizza, First Take, Friday Night Fights, and ESPN Sports Saturday on ABC.

Sara Walsh | Sara Elizabeth Walsh (born April 12, 1978) is an American sportscaster who joined ESPN in May 2010.  Walsh came to ESPN from WUSA-TV in Washington, D.C., where she served as the station’s weekend sports anchor and Redskins beat reporter from 2006 to 2010.  Prior to WUSA, Walsh worked at WKRN in Nashville from 2003 to 2006, winning four regional Emmys in three years.  She co-hosted the weekly “Monday Night Live” with Titans Coach Jeff Fisher, and hosted a weekly radio show with then Titans defensive tackle Albert Haynesworth.  Walsh also served as Sports Director at WPGA in Macon, Georgia from 2001 to 2003, and began her career as a sports writer for the "Beaches Leader" newspaper in Jacksonville Beach.  She was most recently an anchor on ESPN's "SportsCenter" until May 4, 2017, when she was let go by the network.
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [1] — Robert Flores "hosted the live afternoon edition of 'SportsCenter' from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross." (quoted fragment: "replaced with John Buccigross")
- Fact: [2] — Chris McKendry "a role she has served since 1996." (quoted fragment: "since 1996") — establishes co-anchor's ESPN tenure but not the replacement's start year.
- Fact: [1] — Flores "Joining the network in 2005" and was an anchor for SportsCenter 2007–2016 (paraphrase).

Bridge entity and missing/ambiguous facts:
- Bridge entity: John Buccigross (named in [1]). Missing fact: the passages do NOT state "John Buccigross has been an anchor for ESPN since [year]" — that specific start-year is absent.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve the missing fact:
1) Fetch the "John Buccigross" Wikipedia page (lead/infobox and Career section). Extract the exact phrase or data: "has been an anchor for ESPN since [year]" or "joined ESPN in [year]" (lead sentence / infobox / career-start date).
2) If Wikipedia is unavailable or ambiguous, fetch John Buccigross's ESPN biography/profile or ESPN Press Room page. Extract employment start year or statement of how long he's been an anchor at ESPN.
3) As backup, fetch a reliable news/biography source (e.g., major sports-news profile or archived ESPN bio) and extract the earliest cited year indicating when Buccigross began anchoring at ESPN.

No final answer provided here because the required anchor-start year for John Buccigross is not present in the supplied passages.

Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).  Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.  He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".  Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.  He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.  Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.

ESPN Books | ESPN Books is a publishing company operated by ESPN Started in 2004, ESPN Books has published almost 20 books.  ESPN Books also is in charge of producing ESPN's yearly sports encyclopedia.  It also controls its own book club and in addition it ranks the top selling sports books in ESPN Borders.  Since 2008, it has co-published its books with Ballantine Books.

Andrew Marchand | Andrew Marchand is an American senior sports writer for ESPN New York.  Before his professional career, Andrew earned his Bachelor's Degree in Journalism from Ithaca College.  Before joining ESPN.  Andrew spent 9 years as a reporter for the New York Post.  He is a regular contributor to ESPN programs such as Sportscenter, Baseball Tonight, ESPN News, and ESPN New York radio broadcasts.

Jeffri Chadiha | Jeffri Chadiha is an American sports journalist currently employed by NFL Media, having joined the Media arm of the NFL in August 2015.  He previously worked for ESPN, frequently contributing to ESPN.com on the NFL.  Prior to working for ESPN, he worked for Sports Illustrated, having started out at the Ann Arbor News and San Francisco Examiner.  He graduated from the University of Michigan in 1993 after transferring from the University of Wyoming.

Suzy Kolber | Suzanne Lisa "Suzy" Kolber ( ; born May 14, 1964) is an American football sideline reporter, co-producer, and sportscaster for ESPN.  She was one of the original anchors of ESPN2 when it launched in 1993.  Three years later, she left ESPN2 to join Fox Sports, and rejoined ESPN in late 1999.

2017 in Glory | The year 2017 is the sixth year in the history of the Glory, an international kickboxing event.  The year started with .  The events are broadcasts through television agreements with ESPN and other regional channels around the world.
